description: This figure plots saturation magnetization (4πJₛ, unit: G) as a function of carbon content (range: 0.8 to 1.06 wt%) for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic steels with fixed 9 wt% Al and 30 wt% Mn, aged under different conditions to evaluate austenite aging intensity. Curves correspond to samples aged at temperatures from 350 °C to 550 °C for either 16 h (dashed lines) or 100 h (solid lines). Key trends: saturation magnetization rises with increasing C content for all aging conditions above 350 °C, as higher C content promotes precipitation of magnetic κ-phase during aging. For equivalent aging duration, magnetization first increases with aging temperature up to 400–450 °C, then decreases at 500–550 °C; longer aging time yields higher magnetization at the same temperature above 350 °C, indicating more extensive κ-phase precipitation. Samples aged at 350 °C show negligible magnetization irrespective of duration, confirming minimal aging occurs at this temperature. This figure supports the conclusion that carbon is the leading diffusion-mobile element driving the onset of austenite aging in this alloy system, with saturation magnetization serving as a qualitative proxy for aging process intensity.
